elements.sent-2411-002: The paper studies sufficient computable criteria for nonpositivity of superoperators in one-parameter families.

elements.sent-2411-039: This paper initiates a systematic study of the problem of positivity and nonpositivity of superoperators in these families.
elements.sent-2411-040: In a series of articles, we aim to give computable criteria for determining which superoperators in one-parameter families are positive or nonpositive.
elements.sent-2411-041: By computable criterion (or effective condition), we mean a procedure employing only finite number of arithmetic operations.
elements.sent-2411-042: In other words, a computable criterion is an algorithm employing arithmetic operations on real or complex numbers.
elements.sent-2411-043: We refer the reader to \cite{JP1,JP2,Pa for important examples of such criteria in the context of quantum channels.}
elements.sent-2411-044: In the present paper we focus on general one-parameter families of a special form.
elements.sent-2411-045: Namely, we assume that the functions $\alpha_{r}$ are real polynomials in variable $t$ (without real roots) and the matrices $A_{r}$ have complex polynomial functions as coefficients, see Definition 4.1 for the precise statement.
elements.sent-2411-046: We call these families one-parameter families of hermiticity-preserving superoperators (without the adjective general).
elements.sent-2411-047: The aim of the paper is to give sufficient computable criteria for existence of nonpositive superoperators in one-parameter families.
elements.sent-2411-048: Here we are concerned with three main cases: $(1)$ every $\Phi_{t}$ is not positive, $(2)$ $\Phi_{t}$ is not positive for $t$ in some open interval $(u,v)\subseteq\RR$ and $(3)$ there is some $\Phi_{t}$ which is not positive.
elements.sent-2411-049: A one-parameter family is called globally nonpositive in the first case, locally nonpositive in the case of $(2)$ and pointwise nonpositive in the last case, see Definition 4.2.
elements.sent-2411-050: Our methods are based on two constructive mathematical tools: the Descartes rule of signs and the Sturm-Tarski theorem.
elements.sent-2411-051: The Descartes rule of signs (see Theorem 3.1) relates the number of positive real roots of a real univariate polynomial with the number of sign variations in the sequence of signs associated with coefficients of the polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-052: This sequence is called the sign sequence of a univariate polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-053: The Sturm-Tarski theorem (see Theorem 6.2) allows us to calculate the number of distinct real roots of real polynomials under some additional conditions (see Theorem 6.3 and Proposition 6.5).
elements.sent-2411-054: In \cite{Ja3 the author applies the Descartes rule to give a handy criterion for nonpositivity of a fixed hermiticity-preserving superoperator (recalled in Corollary 3.3).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Here we adapt the results from \cite{Ja3 to the case of one-parameter families.}
elements.sent-2411-056: For this purpose, we introduce the sign variation formulas which are generalizations of sign sequences for bivariate polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-057: It turns out that global, local and pointwise nonpositivity of a one-parameter family can be checked by looking at conditions of the form $\forall_{t\in\RR\snull}\varphi(t)$, $\exists_{t\in(u,v)\snull}\varphi(t)$ and $\exists_{t\in\RR\snull}\varphi(t)$, respectively, where $\varphi(t)$ is a sign variation formula of a special form (see Theorem 5.5).
elements.sent-2411-058: Using the Sturm-Tarski theorem we prove that these conditions are computable.
elements.sent-2411-059: This fact is shown in Theorem 6.7 which is the first main result of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-060: The second main result is Theorem 7.1, presenting the computable criterion for nonpositivity of one-parameter families.
